kaarthi_saravanan.name: Kaarthi Saravanan
kaarthi_saravanan.summary:
Kaarthi Saravanan is just getting started on the junior circuit with 2 tournament entries. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Kaarthi's.

Kaarthi competes in both singles and mixed doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.
kaarthi_saravanan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kaarthi Saravanan, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U17 level. A player with 2 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Kaarthi is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 SGVBC SoCal CRC, and so far, the only thing Kaarthi has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Kaarthi saga began at 2025 SGVBC SoCal CRC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kaarthi has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kaarthi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2025 SGVBC SoCal CRC in BS U17, which means Kaarthi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Kaarthi is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Kaarthi wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Kaarthi, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

kaavya_chaudhary.name: Kaavya Chaudhary
kaavya_chaudhary.summary:
Hailing from Texas, Kaavya Chaudhary has shown real competitive teeth, currently competing at the U17 level, breaking into the semifinals 2 times. Across 20 entries in 7 tournaments, every deep run is a signal of growing strength.

A true triple-threat, Kaavya competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a doubles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

Kaavya has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 9 different players across the career:
  • Kwong TO Wong (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #3)

Kaavya has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NorCal, South, Kaavya isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Kaavya's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
kaavya_chaudhary.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kaavya Chaudhary, from Texas, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Kaavya has 20 career entries across 7 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Kaavya saga began at 2022 NorCal ORC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kaavya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 20 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Kaavya has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Kaavya's texts. The go-to partner is Anvitha Nagathan (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Sanjay Chellamuthu has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Kaavya has shown up 3 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Kaavya has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kaavya has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kaavya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U17, which means Kaavya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Kaavya entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Kaavya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Kaavya keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Kaavya has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Kaavya, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Kaavya. We will be here, taking notes.

Kabir competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

A veteran of the junior circuit, Kabir has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Kabir brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
kabir_madiman.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kabir Madiman, from Washington, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Kabir has 14 career entries across 9 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Kabir saga began at 2022 NW Oregon OLC with 2 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

On the doubles front, Kabir has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-25 exit at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U13 — we hope Akhil Chendur has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Kabir has shown up 2 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Kabir has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Kabir has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kabir has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kabir showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U13, which means Kabir traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Kabir entered 2 events with results ranging from 25th to 65th. That is an average placement of 45th, which is a polite way of saying Kabir paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 14 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Kabir. Kabir just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Kabir has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Kabir. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Kabir: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

kai_chong.name: Kai Chong
kai_chong.summary:
Out of Massachusetts, Kai Chong has reached the pinnacle of American junior badminton — selected for the U.S. Junior National Team. Winning at the Selection Event (U17 XD, U17 BD) is the ultimate validation: it means being chosen to represent the country on the international stage. With 8 career titles across 16 tournaments, this is a player who delivers when everything is on the line.

Notably, Kai has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Kai competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, 4 doubles titles, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event champion (U17 XD, U17 BD) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals top-3 (XD U17, BD U17) • 7 ORC titles.

With 10 different doubles partners over the years, Kai adapts to anyone:
  • Jeffrey Chang (current primary partner) — 8 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (8 total events in BD/U19, across 2 seasons)
  • Stella Pan (current primary partner) — 7 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (7 total events in U19/XD, across 2 seasons)
  • Matthew Li — 1 events, 1 title together in BD

Kai has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Kai has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 13 seeded entries across the career, Kai consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 4 seasons and 8 titles, Kai has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
kai_chong.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kai Chong, from Massachusetts, currently competing at the U19 level. Now, before we get started, we do need to acknowledge that Kai is a U.S. Selection Event champion, which means Kai has literally been chosen to represent this country on the international stage. That is an extraordinary accomplishment and Kai would very much like you to stop reading right here. Unfortunately for Kai, we kept reading — all 47 entries across 16 tournaments, to be exact — and what we found behind that one shiny title is a 17% career win rate. Let that number sink in. For every time Kai held up a trophy, there were roughly 4 other times Kai walked out of the gym carrying nothing but a racket bag, a participation wristband, and the quiet dignity of someone pretending it was 'a good learning experience.'

The Kai saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) in BD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Kai's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 4 titles in 17 entries — a stretch where Kai actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 2 wins in 9 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Kai experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Kai has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Kai does own 8 actual titles, so it is not like Kai has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 8 wins means Kai is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kai plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Kai is 0-for-13. Zero titles in 13 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Kai and suddenly the wins start appearing — 7 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Kai needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Kai has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Kai's texts. The go-to partner is Jeffrey Chang (8 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U17 — we hope Annabelle Zhang has emotionally recovered.

Junior Nationals has seen Kai 3 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

Kai has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Kai is not just losing locally — Kai is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 17% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chong family travels cross-country so Kai can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kai has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kai showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U17, which means Kai traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 SoCal ORC in BS U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Kai entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Kai paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 8 titles to the name, 47 entries, and 39 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Kai looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Kai's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Kai has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Kai. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC, where Kai entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. kaishi_yang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kaishi Yang, from Washington, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-7. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Kaishi has 39 career entries across 15 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 5% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Kaishi saga began at 2024 Frisco South ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2025 OBA NW OLC in BS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Kaishi's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kaishi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 1 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Kaishi can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 13 of them, to be precise. Kaishi has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Kaishi's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Kira Chu: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Kira Chu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U11 — we hope Nina Kuan has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Kaishi's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Kaishi has shown up 6 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Kaishi is not just losing locally — Kaishi is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NE, NW, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Yang family travels cross-country so Kaishi can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-49 finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kaishi has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kaishi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 49th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U11, which means Kaishi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Kaishi entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 49th. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Kaishi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 39 entries, and 37 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Kaishi looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Kaishi's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Kaishi has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Kaishi. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kaitlyn Kim, from Illinois, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 59 career entries in 20 tournaments, Kaitlyn has 6 titles — a 10% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 8 entries where Kaitlyn drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 59 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Kaitlyn saga began at 2022 Egret Midwest CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2025 Egret Midwest OLC in XD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Kaitlyn's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 6 titles in 21 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Kaitlyn experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Kaitlyn has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2024 Midwest CRC, where Kaitlyn entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U15, GD U15). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. Now yes, Kaitlyn does own 6 actual titles, so it is not like Kaitlyn has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 6 wins means Kaitlyn is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kaitlyn plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 1 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Kaitlyn can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 23 of them, to be precise. Kaitlyn has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Kaitlyn's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Rudransh Majumdar: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Rudransh Majumdar has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Aaron Lin has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Kaitlyn's 6 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Kaitlyn entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Kaitlyn's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Kaitlyn and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Kaitlyn has shown up 6 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

One thing nobody can accuse Kaitlyn of is playing it safe. Kaitlyn has entered older age groups at 3 different tournaments, spanning U13, U15, U17, U19 — which means Kaitlyn has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 6 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Kaitlyn has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Kaitlyn is not just losing locally — Kaitlyn is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 10% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Kim family travels cross-country so Kaitlyn can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kaitlyn has 24 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kaitlyn showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Kaitlyn traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Kaitlyn entered 3 events with results ranging from 25th to 49th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Kaitlyn paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 6 titles to the name, 59 entries, and 53 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Kaitlyn looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Kaitlyn's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Kaitlyn has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Kaitlyn. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

What makes Kaiwen especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Kaiwen has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

We will be here, taking notes.

kaizhou_zheng.roast:

What makes Kalea especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #3, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior bronze medalist (GS U17, 2019) • U.S. Selection Event top-3 (U19 GD) • Junior Nationals top-2 (GD U19) • 1 ORC title.

In doubles, Kalea has formed effective partnerships:
  • Andre Chim (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U19/XD (6 events, best: #2)
  • Joline Siu (current primary partner) — 5 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (5 total events in GD/U19, across 2 seasons)

The 2021-2022 season tells a story of acceleration, with 1 title across 6 tournaments — a clear sign that Kalea is still ascending.

Kalea has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Kalea has competed across 4 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Kalea brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.

With 7 seeded entries across the career, Kamalini consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 6 seasons of competitive experience, Kameran brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
kameran_hossain.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kameran Hossain, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 69 career entries in 26 tournaments, Kameran has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 68 entries where Kameran drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 69 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Kameran saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 7th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 6 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kameran plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 69 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Kameran has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Kameran's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Tsun Wang Lam: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Tsun Wang Lam has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 NorCal ORC in BD U17 — we hope Yves Judson Homayoon has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Kameran entered 8 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Kameran's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Kameran and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Kameran has shown up 8 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Kameran is not just losing locally — Kameran is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Hossain family travels cross-country so Kameran can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kameran has 42 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kameran showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U17, which means Kameran traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Kameran entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Kameran paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 6 seasons, 69 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Kameran. Kameran just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Kameran has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Kameran. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Kameran: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

kandice_chu.name: Kandice Chu
kandice_chu.summary:
Hailing from California, Kandice Chu has claimed a championship, currently competing at the U15 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 33 entries across 13 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

A true triple-threat, Kandice competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

With 9 different doubles partners over the years, Kandice adapts to anyone:
  • Ryan Li (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (11 events, best: #3, spanning 4 seasons)

Having moved up to U15, Kandice is navigating tougher competition at the higher age group. The adjustment is natural — and with the pedigree Kandice brings, a return to the top is a matter of when, not if.

Kandice has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning NE, NorCal, SoCal, Kandice isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Over 4 seasons and 1 title, Kandice has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
kandice_chu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kandice Chu, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 1 win across 33 entries in 13 tournaments. That is a 3% conversion rate, which means roughly 10 out of every 10 times Kandice shows up to a tournament, Kandice pays the entry fee, laces up the shoes, does the warm-up jog, stretches the hamstrings, steps on court... and then drives home with absolutely nothing to show for it except sore legs and a lighter wallet.

The Kandice saga began at 2022 SoCal CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 3rd. The first title would not come until 2025 YONEX Dave Freeman Jr. SoCal OLC in GS U13, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Kandice's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 1 title in 10 entries — a stretch where Kandice actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Kandice is 0-for-6. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Kandice experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Kandice has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (1 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Kandice does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Kandice has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 1 wins means Kandice is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kandice plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. All 1 title came in singles — the discipline where nobody else can mess things up. In doubles? Zero titles. In mixed? Also zero. Either Kandice is a lone wolf who thrives in isolation, or — and this is our working theory — nobody wants to be the partner who has to explain the loss at dinner. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Kandice has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Kandice's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Ryan Li: 11 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 11 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Ryan Li has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in XD U13 — we hope Ryan Li has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Kandice's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA.

Kandice has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kandice has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kandice showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in GS U13, which means Kandice traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Kandice entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 33rd. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Kandice paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 33 entries, and 32 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Kandice looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Kandice's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Kandice has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Kandice. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Definitely.

karena_ling.name: Karena Ling
karena_ling.summary:
Representing California, Karena Ling is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-4 finish at Selection places Karena in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Karena is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a doubles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-4 (U17 GD) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

Karena has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 14 different players across the career:
  • Sai Sahasra Gajula (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U17 (3 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Kevin Zhang — a trusted U17/XD partner (4 events)
  • Andy W Gao — 2 events in XD, best: #3

A veteran of the junior circuit, Karena has competed across U13, U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Karena has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience, Karena brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
karena_ling.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Karena Ling, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-4. Top-4 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 58 career entries in 21 tournaments, Karena has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 57 entries where Karena drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 58 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Karena saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best of 4th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 5 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Karena would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 5 times Karena has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 5 times Karena has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Karena's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Karena plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 58 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Karena has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Karena's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Leheng Xu: 9 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 9 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Leheng Xu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Leheng Xu has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Karena entered 3 events with results ranging from top-4 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Karena's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Karena and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Karena has shown up 9 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Karena is not just losing locally — Karena is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Ling family travels cross-country so Karena can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Karena has 34 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Karena showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Karena traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 Midwest ORC, where Karena entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Karena paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 58 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Karena. Karena just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Karena has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Karena. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Karena: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

kartik_kanchi.name: Kartik Kanchi
kartik_kanchi.summary:
A North Carolina competitor, Kartik Kanchi knows the feeling of standing atop the podium, currently competing at the U13 level, a moment that separates contenders from champions. With 30 entries across 16 tournaments, the competitive foundation is built — and more titles are within reach.

A true triple-threat, Kartik competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (4 entries).

Kartik has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 10 different players across the career:
  • Adithya Yeluguri — 3 events, 1 title together in BD

Kartik has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Kartik isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Over 4 seasons and 1 title, Kartik has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
kartik_kanchi.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kartik Kanchi, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-7. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Kartik has 30 career entries across 16 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 3% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Kartik saga began at 2023 South CRC with 1 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2024 South CRC (TBTT) in BD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Kartik's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 1 title in 14 entries — a stretch where Kartik actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Kartik is 0-for-4. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kartik plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Kartik is 0-for-16. Zero titles in 16 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Kartik and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Kartik needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Kartik has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Kartik's texts. The go-to partner is Adithya Yeluguri (3 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BD U13 — we hope Lohith Reddy Arava has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Kartik's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Kartik has shown up 4 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Kartik has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kartik has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kartik showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in BS U13, which means Kartik traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 NVBC NE ORC (U13/U15) in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai, where Kartik entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Kartik paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 30 entries, and 29 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Kartik looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Kartik's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Kartik has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Kartik. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

kason_wang.name: Kason Wang
kason_wang.summary:
A Texas competitor, Kason Wang is steadily building a competitive resume, currently competing at the U11 level, with 9 entries across 3 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

Kason is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Kason has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Meera Bhalekar (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events)
kason_wang.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kason Wang, from Texas, currently competing at the U11 level. A player with 9 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Kason is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 Austin Leander South OLC, and so far, the only thing Kason has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Kason saga began at 2025 Austin Leander South OLC with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kason plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 9 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Kason has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kason has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kason showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 Austin Leander South OLC in BS U11, which means Kason traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2025 Schafer SLG South OLC (2025-26) in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 Schafer SLG South OLC (2025-26), where Kason entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 22nd, which is a polite way of saying Kason paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Kason is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Kason wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Kason, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

katelin_hanna_ngo.name: Katelin Hanna Ngo
katelin_hanna_ngo.summary:
Katelin Hanna Ngo is a Pan American Junior Championships bronze medalist, earning a podium finish representing the USA against the best young players in the Western Hemisphere (Girl's Singles, Mixed Doubles). A top-3 finish at the Pan Am Juniors puts Katelin in rarified international company.

Katelin competes in both singles and mixed doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior bronze medalist (Girl's Singles, Mixed Doubles, 2021).
katelin_hanna_ngo.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Katelin Hanna Ngo, from parts unknown. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Katelin is a Pan American Junior Championships bronze medalist (Girl's Singles, Mixed Doubles), which means Katelin has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Katelin's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. But here is the thing about a bronze medal: it means Katelin went all the way to an international championship and STILL did not win. Katelin represented an entire country and came home with the consolation prize. Across 2 career entries and a 0% win rate, the Pan Am bronze is the crown jewel of a résumé that is otherwise... well, let's just say we have 2 entries worth of material.

The Katelin saga began at 2021 Pan Am Junior Selection with 2 entries and a best of 3rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Katelin is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Katelin wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Katelin, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

katheen_li.name: Katheen Li
katheen_li.summary:
A California competitor, Katheen Li has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U17 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Katheen's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Katheen is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

What makes Katheen especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

Katheen has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Vivian Shi (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (4 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Muyang Li (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #5)

Katheen has competed at both U15 and U17, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning NW, NorCal, SoCal, Katheen isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Katheen's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
katheen_li.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Katheen Li, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-5. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Katheen has 12 career entries across 4 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Katheen saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Katheen plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 12 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

At Junior Nationals, Katheen has shown up 3 times with a best of top-5. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-5.

Katheen has also played up into older age groups at 3 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Katheen has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Katheen has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Katheen showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U17, which means Katheen traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Katheen entered 3 events with results ranging from 4th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 15th, which is a polite way of saying Katheen paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Katheen keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Katheen has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Katheen, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Katheen. We will be here, taking notes.

katherine_chiang.name: Katherine Chiang
katherine_chiang.summary:
Hailing from California, Katherine Chiang is writing the opening pages of a badminton story, currently competing at the U13 level, with 5 entries across 2 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

A true triple-threat, Katherine competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.
