katherine_f_xu.name: Katherine F Xu
katherine_f_xu.summary:
Out of California, Katherine F Xu has conquered the Open Regional Championship stage, currently competing at the U15 level, proving the ability to triumph against the strongest regional fields. Open Regional Championships draw the top talent from across the region, and taking home the title is a genuine achievement.

Notably, Katherine has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Katherine especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 4 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (2 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries) • 1 ORC title.

The statistics paint a vivid picture — Katherine boasts 30 straight-games victories, showing the ability to close out matches decisively, a positive +258 point differential across the career, a clutch 57% win rate in three-game matches (8/14).

Katherine has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 32 different players across the career:
  • Kaitlyn Kim (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (4 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Bella Liu (current primary partner) — an ongoing partnership that keeps producing results: 2 titles across 9 events in GD, spanning 3 seasons
  • Nathaniel Volfbeyn (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Xi Cao (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

Having moved up to U17, Katherine is navigating tougher competition at the higher age group. The adjustment is natural — and with the pedigree Katherine brings, a return to the top is a matter of when, not if.

Katherine has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Katherine has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Over 5 seasons and 5 titles, Katherine has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
katherine_f_xu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Katherine F Xu, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 88 career entries in 31 tournaments, Katherine has 5 titles — a 6% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 16 entries where Katherine drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 88 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Katherine saga began at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2022 SoCal Arena CRC in GS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Katherine's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 2 titles in 28 entries — a stretch where Katherine actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Katherine is 0-for-17. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Katherine experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Katherine has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Katherine does own 5 actual titles, so it is not like Katherine has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 5 wins means Katherine is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Katherine plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 4 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Katherine can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 32 of them, to be precise. Katherine has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Katherine's texts. The go-to partner is Bella Liu (9 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in GD U15 — we hope Kaitlyn Kim has emotionally recovered.

Of Katherine's 5 career titles, 4 came at local events versus 1 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Katherine feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Katherine entered 2 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Katherine's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Katherine and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Katherine has shown up 9 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Katherine has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Katherine is not just losing locally — Katherine is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 6% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Xu family travels cross-country so Katherine can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Katherine has 35 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Katherine showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U13, which means Katherine traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Katherine entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Katherine paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 5 titles to the name, 88 entries, and 83 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Katherine looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Katherine's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Katherine has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Katherine. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals, where Katherine entered 3 events with results ranging from 13th to 33rd. kathleen_li.name: Kathleen Li
kathleen_li.summary:
Out of California, Kathleen Li is a Junior Nationals finalist — one match away from the biggest title in American junior badminton. Reaching the finals at Nationals (GS U17, GD U17) puts Kathleen in an incredibly select group, and the experience of competing at that level is invaluable.

What makes Kathleen especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (7 entries) • Junior Nationals top-2 (GS U17, GD U17) • 1 ORC title.

With 10 different doubles partners over the years, Kathleen adapts to anyone:
  • Angela Ye (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD/U19 (3 events, best: #5)
  • Rubie Liu — a trusted GD/U17 partner (8 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Ben Tirtohadi — a trusted U17/XD partner (4 events, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Kathleen has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Kathleen has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 8 seeded entries across the career, Kathleen consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 4 seasons and 1 title, Kathleen has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
kathleen_li.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kathleen Li, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 38 career entries in 15 tournaments, Kathleen has 1 title — a 3% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 37 entries where Kathleen drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 38 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Kathleen saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GD U19, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Kathleen's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 1 title in 12 entries — a stretch where Kathleen actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Kathleen is 0-for-4. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Kathleen experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Kathleen has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Kathleen does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Kathleen has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 1 wins means Kathleen is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kathleen plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Kathleen is 0-for-11. Zero titles in 11 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Kathleen and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Kathleen needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Kathleen has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Kathleen's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Rubie Liu: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Rubie Liu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U19 — we hope Ben Tirtohadi has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Kathleen entered 7 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Kathleen's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Kathleen and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals — the Super Bowl of this sport — Kathleen has competed 3 times, reaching the final once and coming away without the title. So close to being a national champion, and yet so definitively not one. Then there is the seeding situation. Kathleen has been seeded in 8 events and underperformed the seeding 4 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Kathleen takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #2 at 2023 U.S. Selection Event in U17 GS, and finishing 17th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Kathleen. The evidence suggests they should not have.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Kathleen is not just losing locally — Kathleen is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Li family travels cross-country so Kathleen can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kathleen has 8 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kathleen showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U17, which means Kathleen traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Kathleen entered 2 events with results ranging from 2nd to 65th. That is an average placement of 33rd, which is a polite way of saying Kathleen paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 38 entries, and 37 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Kathleen looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Kathleen's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Kathleen has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Kathleen. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 79 career entries in 27 tournaments, Katie has 1 title — a 1% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 78 entries where Katie drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 79 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Katie saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in XD U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Katie's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 1 title in 20 entries — a stretch where Katie actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Katie is 0-for-17. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Katie experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Katie has reached 5 championship matches across this career. That is 5 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 5 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2026 Bintang NC Open Local Championships, where Katie entered 2 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U19, GD U19). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Katie does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Katie has never won anything. But 5 finals losses versus 1 wins means Katie is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Katie plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Katie is 0-for-22. Zero titles in 22 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Katie and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Katie needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 26 of them, to be precise. Katie has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Katie's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Jennifer Bangcaya: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Jennifer Bangcaya has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Justin Liaw has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Katie entered 9 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Katie's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Katie and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Katie 6 times, with a best of top-4. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any.

Katie has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Katie is not just losing locally — Katie is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 1% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wong family travels cross-country so Katie can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Katie has 26 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Katie showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Katie traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Katie entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 46th, which is a polite way of saying Katie paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 79 entries, and 78 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Katie looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Katie's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Katie has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Katie. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

A true triple-threat, Kaustub competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Kaustub has built chemistry with several doubles partners:
  • Napat Srisalaipaisal (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #2)

Kaustub has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Kaustub has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Kaustub's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
kaustub_gautam.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kaustub Gautam, from Washington, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-7. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Kaustub has 12 career entries across 6 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Kaustub saga began at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 2 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kaustub plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 12 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

At Junior Nationals, Kaustub has shown up 2 times with a best of top-7. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-7.

Kaustub has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Kaustub is not just losing locally — Kaustub is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Gautam family travels cross-country so Kaustub can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kaustub has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kaustub showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in BS U17, which means Kaustub traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Kaustub entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 23rd, which is a polite way of saying Kaustub paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Kaustub keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Kaustub has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Kaustub, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Kaustub. We will be here, taking notes.

kavin_elangovan.name: Kavin Elangovan
kavin_elangovan.summary: Out of Connecticut, Kavin Elangovan has stepped onto the competitive stage with 1 tournament entry. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Kavin's.
kavin_elangovan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kavin Elangovan, from Connecticut, currently competing at the U13 level. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Kavin is right on schedule. It all started at 2022 NE CRC, and so far, the only thing Kavin has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Kavin saga began at 2022 NE CRC with 1 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Kavin is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Kavin's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Kavin is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kavin has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kavin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2022 NE CRC in BS U13, which means Kavin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

Kavin is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Kavin wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Kavin, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

kavinmadhi_malar.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kavinmadhi Malar, from Washington, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-25. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Kavinmadhi has 15 career entries across 6 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Kavinmadhi saga began at 2021 HPBC NW ORC with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kavinmadhi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 15 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Kavinmadhi has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Kavinmadhi's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U13 — we hope Brendan E Chen has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Kavinmadhi has shown up 3 times with a best of top-25. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kavinmadhi has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kavinmadhi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2021 HPBC NW ORC in XD U13, which means Kavinmadhi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Kavinmadhi entered 3 events with results ranging from 25th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 30th, which is a polite way of saying Kavinmadhi paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 15 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Kavinmadhi. Kavinmadhi just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Kavinmadhi has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Kavinmadhi. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Kavinmadhi: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

kavya_balaji.name: Kavya Balaji
kavya_balaji.summary:
Out of Texas, Kavya Balaji is a Junior Nationals finalist — one match away from the biggest title in American junior badminton. Reaching the finals at Nationals (GS U11, GD U11) puts Kavya in an incredibly select group, and the experience of competing at that level is invaluable.

Kavya is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Kavya is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 3 singles titles, 3 doubles titles, 4 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals top-2 (GS U11, GD U11) • 5 ORC titles.

Beyond the trophies, the numbers stand out: Kavya boasts 47 straight-games victories, showing the ability to close out matches decisively, a commanding 76% win rate across 78 matches, a clutch 59% win rate in three-game matches (10/17).

Kavya has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 14 different players across the career:
  • Laasya Polineni (current primary partner) — 6 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (6 total events in GD, across 2 seasons)
  • Shrihayagrivan Karthigayan (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (4 total events in XD)
  • Eeshani Devasena Josyula (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in GD, across 2 seasons
  • Varish Karri (current partner) — 2 events in XD, producing 2 titles together, across 2 seasons

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 5 podium finishes across 5 tournaments. Kavya is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Kavya has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Kavya has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Kavya has been seeded #1 or #2 in 6 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Over 4 seasons and 10 titles, Kavya has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
kavya_balaji.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kavya Balaji, from Texas, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-2. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Kavya has 57 career entries across 20 tournaments and has managed to win 10 of them — a 18% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Kavya saga began at 2022 South Houston CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2024 Capital NE ORC (U11/U17/U19) in GD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Kavya's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 6 titles in 24 entries — a stretch where Kavya actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 4 wins in 15 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Kavya experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Kavya has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals, where Kavya entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (GS U11, GD U11). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Kavya does own 10 actual titles, so it is not like Kavya has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 10 wins means Kavya is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kavya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 3 doubles, 4 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Kavya can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Kavya has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Kavya's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Srihari Shyam: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Srihari Shyam has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in GD U11 — we hope Chaarvi Telikuntla has emotionally recovered.

Junior Nationals has been particularly cruel: 6 entries, 2 finals appearances, and zero titles. Kavya has stood on the biggest stage in American junior badminton, one match from the most important title in the sport, and come up short 2 times. That is the kind of heartbreak that builds character — or destroys it. Time will tell. Tournament committees have handed Kavya a #1 or #2 seed 6 times, and Kavya has failed to live up to it in 2 of them — including at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in GS U11, where Kavya was seeded #2 and finished 5th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

Kavya has also played up into older age groups at 4 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Kavya is not just losing locally — Kavya is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 18% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Balaji family travels cross-country so Kavya can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kavya has 10 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kavya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U11, which means Kavya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in GS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Kavya entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 19th, which is a polite way of saying Kavya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 10 titles to the name, 57 entries, and 47 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the ORC victories, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Kavya looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Kavya's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Kavya has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Kavya. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kavya has 33 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kavya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U13, which means Kavya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Kavya entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Kavya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 48 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Kavya. Kavya just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Kavya has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Kavya. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Kavya: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

kavya_krishna.name: Kavya Krishna
kavya_krishna.summary:
Hailing from Virginia, Kavya Krishna is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-4 finish at Selection places Kavya in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

Kavya is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (3 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

A true triple-threat, Kavya competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 1 singles title, 2 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-4 (U17 GS) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries) • 3 ORC titles.

Kavya has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 20 different players across the career:
  • Shawn Xia (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #4, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Chris Wu — a trusted U17/XD partner (4 events, best: #3, spanning 3 seasons)

Kavya has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Kavya has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 7 seeded entries across the career, Kavya consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Over 5 seasons and 3 titles, Kavya has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
kavya_krishna.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kavya Krishna, from Virginia, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-4. Top-4 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 57 career entries in 21 tournaments, Kavya has 3 titles — a 5% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 18 entries where Kavya drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 57 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Kavya saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best finish of 7th. The first title would not come until 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in GD U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Kavya's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2022-2023 season — 2 titles in 16 entries — a stretch where Kavya actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Kavya is 0-for-5. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Kavya experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Kavya has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Kavya does own 3 actual titles, so it is not like Kavya has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 3 wins means Kavya is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kavya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 2 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Kavya can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 20 of them, to be precise. Kavya has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Kavya's texts. The go-to partner is Chris Wu (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 South Frisco ORC in XD U17 — we hope Alan Premkumar has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Kavya entered 9 events with results ranging from top-4 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Kavya's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Kavya and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Kavya has shown up 9 times with a best of top-6. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-6. Then there is the seeding situation. Kavya has been seeded in 7 events and underperformed the seeding 3 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Kavya takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #2 at 2024 Capital NE ORC (U11/U17/U19) in GS U19, and finishing 4th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Kavya. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Kavya has also played up into older age groups at 3 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Kavya is not just losing locally — Kavya is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 5% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Krishna family travels cross-country so Kavya can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 South Frisco ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kavya has 17 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kavya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 South Frisco ORC in XD U17, which means Kavya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Kavya entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 17th. That is an average placement of 17th, which is a polite way of saying Kavya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 57 entries, and 54 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Kavya looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Kavya's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Kavya has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Kavya. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

With 5 different doubles partners over the years, Kaydence adapts to anyone:

kaydence_cheung.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kaydence Cheung, from New Jersey, currently competing at the U13 level. A player who has entered 12 events across 5 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 12-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Kaydence's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Kaydence keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Kaydence saga began at 2024 NE CRC (NJBC) with 1 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kaydence plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 12 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Kaydence has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in XD U13 — we hope Ethan Li has emotionally recovered.

Kaydence has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kaydence has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kaydence showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in GS U13, which means Kaydence traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Kaydence entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 27th, which is a polite way of saying Kaydence paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Kaydence keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Kaydence has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Kaydence, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Kaydence. We will be here, taking notes.

kayla_x_wu.name: Kayla X. Wu
kayla_x_wu.summary:
Out of New York, Kayla X. Wu has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U19 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Kayla's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (2 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

With 7 different doubles partners over the years, Kayla adapts to anyone:
  • Haoyang Zheng (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)
  • Aashi Agarwal (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #5)

Kayla has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, NW, Kayla isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

kayla_x_wu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kayla X. Wu, from New York, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-5. Top-5 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 18 career entries in 7 tournaments, Kayla has 1 title — a 6% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 17 entries where Kayla drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 18 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Kayla saga began at 2022 NE CRC, where Kayla burst onto the scene with a title in GS U17 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Kayla has delivered on some of those promises: 1 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Kayla promised and what Kayla has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 1 title in 7 entries — a stretch where Kayla actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Kayla is 0-for-6. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kayla plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. All 1 title came in singles — the discipline where nobody else can mess things up. In doubles? Zero titles. In mixed? Also zero. Either Kayla is a lone wolf who thrives in isolation, or — and this is our working theory — nobody wants to be the partner who has to explain the loss at dinner. On the doubles front, Kayla has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Haoyang Zheng (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in XD U17 — we hope Siva Rajesh has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Kayla's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Kayla entered 2 events with results ranging from top-5 to a humbling round-of-9. The Selection committee looked at Kayla's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Kayla and said 'nah.'

Kayla has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Kayla has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kayla has 4 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kayla showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2022 NE ORC (U15/U17/U19) in XD U17, which means Kayla traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2022 NW ORC in GS U19. A pattern, not a blip.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 18 entries, and 17 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Kayla looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, is still figuring it out, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Kayla's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Kayla has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Kayla. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Kaylie entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Kaylie paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

The sport needs people like Kayosh. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Kayosh: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

  • Fonghong Lin (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (3 events, best: #2)
  • Rachel Yuezhi Xu (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

The 2025-2026 season has been a step up, with 4 titles across 6 tournaments — a clear sign that Keaton is still ascending.

Keaton has competed at both U11 and U13, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Keaton has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 10 seeded entries across the career, Keaton consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 6 titles, Keaton is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
keaton_ou.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Keaton Ou, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Keaton has 56 career entries across 20 tournaments and has managed to win 6 of them — a 11% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Keaton saga began at 2022 SoCal CRC with 3 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2024 SGVBC SoCal CRC in BD U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Keaton's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 4 titles in 17 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Keaton experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Keaton has reached 4 championship matches across this career. That is 4 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 4 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Keaton does own 6 actual titles, so it is not like Keaton has never won anything. But 4 finals losses versus 6 wins means Keaton is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Keaton plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 3 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Keaton can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 17 of them, to be precise. Keaton has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Keaton's texts. The go-to partner is Grace Wang (10 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in XD U13 — we hope Samanvi Shetty has emotionally recovered.

Of Keaton's 6 career titles, 5 came at local events versus 1 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Keaton feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room. At Junior Nationals, Keaton has shown up 6 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9. Tournament committees have handed Keaton a #1 or #2 seed 4 times, and Keaton has failed to live up to it in 2 of them — including at 2024 SGVBC SoCal CRC in BS U11, where Keaton was seeded #1 and finished 5th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Keaton is not just losing locally — Keaton is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 11% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Ou family travels cross-country so Keaton can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Keaton has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Keaton showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BS U13, which means Keaton traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC, where Keaton entered 3 events with results ranging from 5th to 65th. That is an average placement of 34th, which is a polite way of saying Keaton paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 6 titles to the name, 56 entries, and 50 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the ORC victories, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Keaton looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Keaton's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Keaton has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Keaton. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.
