What makes Kehong especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (2 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (4 entries).

With 10 different doubles partners over the years, Kehong adapts to anyone:
  • Aidan Wong (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD/U17 (4 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)

Kehong has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Kehong has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Kehong brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
kehong_ng.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kehong Ng, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 32 career entries in 13 tournaments, Kehong has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 31 entries where Kehong drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 32 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Kehong saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kehong plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 32 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Kehong has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Kehong's texts. The go-to partner is Justin Liaw (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-49 exit at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U17 — we hope Aidan Wong has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Kehong entered 2 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Kehong's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Kehong and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Kehong has shown up 4 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Kehong is not just losing locally — Kehong is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Ng family travels cross-country so Kehong can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-97 finish at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kehong has 24 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kehong showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 97th-place finish at 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U17, which means Kehong traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Junior Nationals, where Kehong entered 2 events with results ranging from 49th to 97th. That is an average placement of 73rd, which is a polite way of saying Kehong paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 32 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Kehong. Kehong just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Kehong has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Kehong. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Kehong: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

keira_winarto.name: Keira Winarto
keira_winarto.summary:
Hailing from North Carolina, Keira Winarto is a Pan American Junior Championships gold medalist — representing the United States on the international stage and coming home with gold. Winning at the Pan Am Junior Championships (XD U13, GD U13) is an elite, continent-level achievement. With 16 career titles across 28 tournaments, this is a player who delivers on the biggest stages.

Keira is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 3 singles titles, 8 doubles titles, 5 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior champion (XD U13, GD U13, 2025) • U.S. Selection Event top-2 (U13 XD, U13 GD, U13 GS) • Junior Nationals champion (XD U13) • 5 ORC titles.

Keira has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 14 different players across the career:
  • Varish Karri (current primary partner) — 12 events together this season alone with 3 titles, building serious momentum (20 total events in U13/XD, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Lilian Feng (current primary partner) — 12 events together this season alone with 4 titles, building serious momentum (18 total events in GD/U13, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Srineet Atluri — 2 events, 1 title together in XD, across 2 seasons
  • Sophia Liu — 3 events, 1 title together in GD

Having moved up to U15, Keira is navigating tougher competition at the higher age group. The adjustment is natural — and with the pedigree Keira brings, a return to the top is a matter of when, not if.

Keira has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Keira has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Keira has been seeded #1 or #2 in 10 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

With 5 seasons of competitive experience and 16 titles, Keira is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.

That is an average placement of 15th, which is a polite way of saying Keira paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

If you squint at the highlights — the Pan Am medal, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Keira looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Keira's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Keira has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Keira. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

kelly_hu.name: Kelly Hu
kelly_hu.summary:
Out of Illinois, Kelly Hu is in the early chapters of a competitive journey, currently competing at the U15 level, with 6 entries across 2 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

What makes Kelly especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.
kelly_hu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kelly Hu, from Illinois, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 6 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Kelly is right on schedule. It all started at 2023 South ORC, and so far, the only thing Kelly has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Kelly saga began at 2023 South ORC with 3 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kelly plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 6 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

Kelly has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kelly has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kelly showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Kelly traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in GS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Kelly entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Kelly paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Kelly is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Kelly wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Kelly, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

kelly_linn_hu.name: Kelly Linn Hu
kelly_linn_hu.summary:
Hailing from Illinois, Kelly Linn Hu has stepped onto the championship stage, currently competing at the U17 level, reaching the finals 1 time. With 39 entries across 16 tournaments, Kelly is proving the ability to compete at the highest level.

A true triple-threat, Kelly competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #3. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (2 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (3 entries).

With 16 different doubles partners over the years, Kelly adapts to anyone:
  • Zoey Tian (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in GD (3 events, best: #3, across 2 seasons)
  • Aaron Fu (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #5, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Kelly has competed across U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Kelly has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Kelly brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
kelly_linn_hu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kelly Linn Hu, from Illinois, currently competing at the U17 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-33. Top-33 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 39 career entries in 16 tournaments, Kelly has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 38 entries where Kelly drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 39 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Kelly saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 3 entries and a best of 2nd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

If there were a trophy for semifinal exits, Kelly would have a dynasty that would make the New England Patriots jealous. 4 times Kelly has fought to the final four — the point where the tournament is supposed to get exciting — and 4 times Kelly has been sent home before the championship match even started. It is like having a GPS programmed to shut off one turn before the destination, every single time. The semifinals should honestly be renamed in Kelly's honor, because nobody has spent more time there without advancing.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kelly plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 39 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Kelly has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Kelly's texts. The go-to partner is Lina Vi Dinh (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2023 South ORC in XD U15 — we hope Stanley Jiaye Song has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Kelly entered 2 events with results ranging from top-33 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Kelly's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Kelly and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Kelly has shown up 3 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Kelly is not just losing locally — Kelly is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Hu family travels cross-country so Kelly can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kelly has 21 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kelly showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in GS U15, which means Kelly traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Kelly entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Kelly paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 39 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Kelly. Kelly just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Kelly has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Kelly. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Kelly: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wu family travels cross-country so Kennedy can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2025 YONEX U.S. Selection Event. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kennedy has 11 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kennedy showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 YONEX U.S. Selection Event in U13 BS, which means Kennedy traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2026 Bintang NC Open Local Championships in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX U.S. Selection Event, where Kennedy entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Kennedy paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 31 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Kennedy. Kennedy just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Kennedy has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Kennedy. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Kennedy: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

kenneth_wu.name: Kenneth Wu
kenneth_wu.summary:
A California competitor, Kenneth Wu is among the elite few to reach the podium at the U.S. Selection Event — the tournament that determines who represents America internationally. A top-3 finish at Selection places Kenneth in the rarest company in U.S. junior badminton.

What makes Kenneth especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting a singles best of #2, 1 doubles title, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event top-3 (U15 BD) • Junior Nationals competitor (9 entries) • ORC top-2 finisher.

Kenneth has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 9 different players across the career:
  • Tao Peng (current primary partner) — 10 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (10 total events in BD/U15, across 2 seasons)
  • Jade Hu (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, across 2 seasons)
  • Saanya Sreenivas (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #5)

The 2024-2025 season tells a story of acceleration, with 1 title across 7 tournaments — a clear sign that Kenneth is still ascending.

Kenneth has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Kenneth has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 1 title, Kenneth is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
kenneth_wu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kenneth Wu, from California, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-3. Top-3 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 51 career entries in 18 tournaments, Kenneth has 1 title — a 2% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 50 entries where Kenneth drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 51 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Kenneth saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 2 entries and a best finish of 2nd. The first title would not come until 2025 CAN-AM Elite NorCal OLC in BD U15 - Position 1-4, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Kenneth's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Kenneth experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Kenneth has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2021 HPBC NW ORC, where Kenneth entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U11, BD U11). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (1 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (2 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Kenneth does own 1 actual title, so it is not like Kenneth has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 1 wins means Kenneth is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kenneth plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. In singles — the purest test of individual skill, where there is nowhere to hide and no one else to blame — Kenneth is 0-for-16. Zero titles in 16 tries. The best singles result is a 2nd-place finish, which is the kind of thing you technically cannot put on a highlight reel. But here is the twist: put a partner on the court alongside Kenneth and suddenly the wins start appearing — 1 titles in doubles and mixed. The data is screaming a very clear message: Kenneth needs help. Alone? Winless. With a teammate? A champion. There is probably a metaphor about teamwork in there somewhere, but honestly it just looks like someone who cannot get the job done solo. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Kenneth has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Kenneth's texts. The go-to partner is Tao Peng (10 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U15 — we hope Amy Gao has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Kenneth's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Kenneth entered 3 events with results ranging from top-3 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Kenneth's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Kenneth and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Kenneth has shown up 9 times with a best of top-5. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-5.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Kenneth is not just losing locally — Kenneth is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 2% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Wu family travels cross-country so Kenneth can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kenneth has 22 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kenneth showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U13, which means Kenneth traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 NorCal ORC, where Kenneth entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 65th. That is an average placement of 35th, which is a polite way of saying Kenneth paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 51 entries, and 50 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Kenneth looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, cannot win a singles match to save their life, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Kenneth's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Kenneth has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Kenneth. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Notably, Kennis has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Kennis competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting a singles best of #4, a doubles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Kennis has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 8 different players across the career:
  • Vivaan Khullar (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, across 2 seasons)

Kennis has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Kennis brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
kennis_he.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kennis He, from Illinois, currently competing at the U15 level. A player who has entered 18 events across 7 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 18-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Kennis's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Kennis keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Kennis saga began at 2022 Egret Midwest CRC with 2 entries and a best of 2nd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Kennis experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Kennis has reached the finals 2 times — at 2022 Egret Midwest CRC, 2023 Midwest OLC — and lost every time. Winning every match in a tournament except the one that actually matters takes a special kind of talent, and Kennis has it in spades.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kennis plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 18 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 8 of them, to be precise. Kennis has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Kennis's texts. The go-to partner is Vivaan Khullar (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Vivaan Khullar has emotionally recovered.

Kennis has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Kennis has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kennis has 6 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kennis showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Kennis traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Kennis entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Kennis paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Kennis keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Kennis has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Kennis, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Kennis. We will be here, taking notes.

Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kesan Murthysundar, from North Carolina, currently competing at the U13 level. A player who has entered 20 events across 9 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 20-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Kesan's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Kesan keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Kesan saga began at 2024 NE OLC (NVBC) with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kesan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 20 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Kesan has cycled through 7 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Tapaswin Gandhavalla (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in BD U13 — we hope Adithya Yeluguri has emotionally recovered.

Kesan has competed across 2 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kesan has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kesan showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BS U13, which means Kesan traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC, where Kesan entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Kesan paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Kesan keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Kesan has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Kesan, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Kesan. We will be here, taking notes.

ketan_dubey.name: Ketan Dubey
ketan_dubey.summary:
Out of Illinois, Ketan Dubey has conquered the Open Regional Championship stage, currently competing at the U13 level, proving the ability to triumph against the strongest regional fields. Open Regional Championships draw the top talent from across the region, and taking home the title is a genuine achievement.

Notably, Ketan has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Ketan is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 3 singles titles, 2 doubles titles, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: 2 ORC titles.

With 6 different doubles partners over the years, Ketan adapts to anyone:
  • Melvin Cui (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 2 titles, building serious momentum (4 total events in BD, across 2 seasons)
  • Janice Chen (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Kennis He (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #2)

Ketan is peaking at the right time in 2022-2023, with 4 titles across 4 tournaments — a clear sign that Ketan is still ascending.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Ketan has competed across U11, U13 and U15, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Ketan isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 5 seeded entries across the career, Ketan consistently earns the respect of tournament organizers.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Ketan's journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
ketan_dubey.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Ketan Dubey, from Illinois, currently competing at the U13 level. An Open Regional Championship titlist with 2 ORC titles, which sounds very official — and it is, ORCs are the real deal. But let's zoom out: that's 2 wins out of 12 ORC entries, and an overall career of 5-for-20 (25%). Regional royalty, national afterthought. A big deal on the local circuit, a footnote everywhere else. Let's dig in.

The Ketan saga began at 2021 Egret Midwest OLC with 2 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2022 Midwest CRC in BS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Ketan's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2022-2023 season has actually been the best yet — 4 titles in 11 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Ketan experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Ketan has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Ketan does own 5 actual titles, so it is not like Ketan has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 5 wins means Ketan is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Ketan plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 2 doubles, 0 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Ketan can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. On the doubles front, Ketan has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The go-to partner is Melvin Cui (4 events, 2 wins), which is not exactly setting the world on fire.

Of Ketan's 5 career titles, 3 came at local events versus 2 at ORCs or higher. That ratio tells you everything: Ketan feasts when the field is weak and struggles when the competition shows up. The résumé is padded the way a freshman pads a college application with 'extracurricular activities' that were really just sitting in a room.

Ketan has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. Ketan has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 5 titles to the name, 20 entries, and 15 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the ORC victories, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Ketan looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, is still figuring it out, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Ketan's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Ketan has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Ketan. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Kevin is right on schedule. It all started at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC, and so far, the only thing Kevin has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Kevin saga began at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kevin has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kevin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BS U11, which means Kevin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC in BD U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX LIBC NE ORC, where Kevin entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Kevin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Kevin is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Kevin wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Kevin, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

kevin_xie.name: Kevin Xie
kevin_xie.summary:
Representing Maryland, Kevin Xie has climbed to the top of the mountain at Junior Nationals, the crown jewel of the American junior badminton season in BD U11. That title represents the culmination of grueling competition against the very best in the country. With 10 career victories across 23 tournaments, Kevin has proven the ability to deliver when it matters most.

Kevin is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Kevin is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 3 singles titles, 5 doubles titles, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals champion (BD U11) • 5 ORC titles.

The statistics paint a vivid picture — Kevin boasts 62 straight-games victories, showing the ability to close out matches decisively, a career +829 point differential, a biggest win margin of 33 points.

With 11 different doubles partners over the years, Kevin adapts to anyone:
  • Anirudh Rudharraju (current primary partner) — an ongoing partnership that keeps producing results: 4 titles across 17 events in BD, spanning 4 seasons
  • Claire QiuChen Li (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in U13/XD (9 events, best: #2, across 2 seasons)
  • Alaina Benjamin (current partner) — 2 events, 1 title together in XD, across 2 seasons
  • Zelenia Shen — 5 events, 1 title together in XD, across 2 seasons

The 2025-2026 season has been outstanding, with 8 podium finishes across 5 tournaments. Kevin is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Kevin has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Kevin has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Kevin has been seeded #1 or #2 in 10 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Over 5 seasons and 10 titles, Kevin has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
kevin_xie.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kevin Xie, from Maryland, currently competing at the U15 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. And yes, Kevin IS a Junior National Champion — BD U11 — which is a legitimate, no-asterisks, put-it-on-the-college-application achievement. We will give that its due respect for about five seconds... okay, time's up. Because behind those headlines is a 64-entry career across 23 tournaments with a 16% win rate, which means 54 times — 54! — Kevin went home without a trophy. So buckle in, because we have 64 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Kevin saga began at 2022 NE CRC with 1 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2023 Midwest OLC in BS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Kevin's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2023-2024 season — 6 titles in 18 entries — a stretch where Kevin actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Kevin is 0-for-15. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Kevin experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Kevin has reached 9 championship matches across this career. That is 9 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 9 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2025 YONEX SPBA Midwest ORC, where Kevin entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (BS U13, BD U13). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (6 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Kevin does own 10 actual titles, so it is not like Kevin has never won anything. But 9 finals losses versus 10 wins means Kevin is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kevin plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 3 singles, 5 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Kevin can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Kevin has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Kevin's texts. The most successful partnership is with Anirudh Rudharraju — 17 events, 4 titles — and honestly, Anirudh Rudharraju deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Anirudh Rudharraju a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U13 — we hope Zelenia Shen has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Kevin entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-9. The Selection committee looked at Kevin's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Kevin and said 'nah.' Then there is the seeding situation. Kevin has been seeded in 18 events and underperformed the seeding 5 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Kevin takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in BS U13, and finishing 9th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Kevin. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Kevin of is playing it safe. Kevin has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15 — which means Kevin has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' The results in those older brackets have been... character-building. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Kevin has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Kevin is not just losing locally — Kevin is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 16% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Xie family travels cross-country so Kevin can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kevin has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kevin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 South ORC in BS U11, which means Kevin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 Synergy NorCal ORC in XD U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Kevin entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Kevin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 10 titles to the name, 64 entries, and 54 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the national title, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Kevin looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Kevin's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Kevin has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Kevin. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (4 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (3 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. And remember: Kevin has ZERO titles. None. 7 times in the final, zero trophies to show for it. This is the most committed bridesmaid in the history of junior badminton.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kevin plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 72 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 15 of them, to be precise. Kevin has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Kevin's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Angelina Lin Wu: 8 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 8 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Angelina Lin Wu has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BD U17 — we hope Kelvin Zhang has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Kevin entered 9 events with results ranging from top-3 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Kevin's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Kevin and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals — the Super Bowl of this sport — Kevin has competed 6 times, reaching the final once and coming away without the title. So close to being a national champion, and yet so definitively not one.

Kevin has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Kevin is not just losing locally — Kevin is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhang family travels cross-country so Kevin can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kevin has 17 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kevin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15, which means Kevin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Kevin entered 3 events with results ranging from 17th to 65th. That is an average placement of 38th, which is a polite way of saying Kevin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 5 seasons, 72 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Kevin. Kevin just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Kevin has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Kevin. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Kevin: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

Kevin plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 2 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Kevin can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 15 of them, to be precise. Kevin has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Kevin's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Vivian Shi: 9 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 9 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Vivian Shi has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in XD U19 — we hope Vivian Shi has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Kevin entered 11 events with results ranging from top-3 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Kevin's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Kevin and said 'nah.' Junior Nationals has seen Kevin 9 times, with a best of top-3. Close enough to the podium to feel the confetti, not close enough to actually catch any. Tournament committees have handed Kevin a #1 or #2 seed 3 times, and Kevin has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2024 Bellevue NW ORC in BS U19, where Kevin was seeded #2 and finished 5th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

Kevin has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Kevin is not just losing locally — Kevin is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 9% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Zhou family travels cross-country so Kevin can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 U.S. Selection Event. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kevin has 7 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kevin showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 U.S. Selection Event in U17 BS, which means Kevin traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2023 SoCal ORC in BS U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 U.S. Selection Event, where Kevin entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Kevin paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 6 titles to the name, 65 entries, and 59 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Kevin looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Kevin's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Kevin has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Kevin. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

kezhen_tao.name: Kezhen Tao
kezhen_tao.summary:
A California competitor, Kezhen Tao is a committed competitor who keeps showing up, currently competing at the U17 level, with 24 career entries across 9 tournaments. In a sport that rewards persistence, Kezhen's commitment is the ultimate investment in future success.

Kezhen is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Kezhen has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 6 different players across the career:
  • Hamilton Le (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (4 events, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Emily Ou — a trusted XD partner (6 events, across 2 seasons)

A veteran of the junior circuit, Kezhen has competed across U13, U15 and U17, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

With tournaments spanning NW, NorCal, SoCal, Kezhen isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience, Kezhen brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
kezhen_tao.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kezhen Tao, from California, currently competing at the U17 level. A player who has entered 24 events across 9 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 24-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Kezhen's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Kezhen keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Kezhen saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kezhen plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 24 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Kezhen has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The longest-running partnership is with Emily Ou: 6 events together, zero titles. Let that marinate. 6 tournaments of shared warm-ups, shared strategies, shared hotel lobbies — and shared losses. That is not a doubles partnership; that is a mutual support group. We hope Emily Ou has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2021 SoCal SGVBC ORC in BD U13 — we hope Jayden Mar has emotionally recovered.

Kezhen has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kezhen has 21 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kezhen showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U15, which means Kezhen traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai, where Kezhen entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Kezhen paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 24 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Kezhen. Kezhen just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Kezhen has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Kezhen. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Kezhen: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.
