kido_phan.name: Kido Phan
kido_phan.summary:
Hailing from California, Kido Phan is on an upward trajectory, currently competing at the U19 level, with 14 entries across 7 tournaments. Every tournament adds experience, every match sharpens the game.

A true triple-threat, Kido competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

Kido has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 6 different players across the career:
  • Aiden Chan (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events)

Kido has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning NE, NorCal, SoCal, Kido isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Kido brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
kido_phan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kido Phan, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-33. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Kido has 14 career entries across 7 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Kido saga began at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai with 3 entries and a best of 33rd, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kido plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 14 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Kido has cycled through 6 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2024 SoCal ORC / Shonai in BD U17 — we hope Seiji Phan has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Kido has shown up 2 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Kido has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kido has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kido showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2025 Arena SoCal ORC in BS U17, which means Kido traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2025 YONEX Synergy NorCal ORC, where Kido entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Kido paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Kido keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Kido has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Kido, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Kido. We will be here, taking notes.

kimaya_krishna.name: Kimaya Krishna
kimaya_krishna.summary:
Out of Virginia, Kimaya Krishna is a Pan American Junior Championships silver medalist, earning a podium finish representing the USA against the best young players in the Western Hemisphere (GD U15). A top-2 finish at the Pan Am Juniors puts Kimaya in rarified international company.

Notably, Kimaya has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

What makes Kimaya especially formidable is the versatility across all three disciplines — boasting 1 singles title, 12 doubles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior silver medalist (GD U15, 2025) • U.S. Selection Event champion (U15 GD, U13 XD, U13 GS, U13 GD) — selected to represent the USA • Junior Nationals champion (GD U11 - Position 1-4, GD U13) • 8 ORC titles.

Kimaya has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 16 different players across the career:
  • Zaph J-Din Bayona (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #4, across 2 seasons)
  • Venkata Shashank Kondaveeti (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (3 events, best: #3, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Aadya Pitani (current partner) — currently teaming up in GD (2 events, best: #5)

Kimaya has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Kimaya has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Kimaya has been seeded #1 or #2 in 9 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Over 6 seasons and 16 titles, Kimaya has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
kimaya_krishna.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kimaya Krishna, from Virginia, currently competing at the U17 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Kimaya is a Pan American Junior Championships silver medalist (GD U15), which means Kimaya has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Kimaya's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. But here is the thing about a silver medal: it means Kimaya went all the way to an international championship and STILL did not win. Kimaya represented an entire country and came home with the consolation prize. Across 86 career entries and a 19% win rate, the Pan Am silver is the crown jewel of a résumé that is otherwise... well, let's just say we have 86 entries worth of material.

The Kimaya saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Kimaya burst onto the scene with a title in GD U11 - Position 1-4 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Kimaya has delivered on some of those promises: 16 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Kimaya promised and what Kimaya has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 5 titles in 15 entries — a stretch where Kimaya actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Kimaya is 0-for-9. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Kimaya experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Kimaya has reached 13 championship matches across this career. That is 13 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 13 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Kimaya entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (GS U11, XD U11). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (3 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (9 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Kimaya does own 16 actual titles, so it is not like Kimaya has never won anything. But 13 finals losses versus 16 wins means Kimaya is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kimaya plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 1 singles, 12 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Kimaya can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Kimaya has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Kimaya's texts. The most successful partnership is with Joanne Li — 19 events, 10 titles — and honestly, Joanne Li deserves hazard pay for carrying this doubles career on their back. Someone send Joanne Li a thank-you card, a gift basket, and maybe a chiropractor referral for all that heavy lifting. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in XD U15 — we hope Seth Li has emotionally recovered.

Then there is the seeding situation. Kimaya has been seeded in 22 events and underperformed the seeding 7 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Kimaya takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15, and finishing 5th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Kimaya. The evidence suggests they should not have.

Kimaya has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Kimaya is not just losing locally — Kimaya is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 19% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Krishna family travels cross-country so Kimaya can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15). Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kimaya has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kimaya showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2023 NE ORC (U13/U15) in XD U15, which means Kimaya traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2026 YONEX Arena SoCal ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2026 YONEX DFW South ORC, where Kimaya entered 3 events with results ranging from 9th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 19th, which is a polite way of saying Kimaya paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 6 seasons deep, 16 titles to the name, 86 entries, and 70 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Pan Am medal, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Kimaya looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Kimaya's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Kimaya has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Kimaya. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

kingsley_chong.name: Kingsley Chong

Hailing from Washington, Kingsley Chong has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-3), currently competing at the U17 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Kingsley's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Kingsley is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 2 singles titles, a doubles best of #2, a mixed doubles best of #2. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (5 entries) • ORC top-3 finisher.

Kingsley has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 14 different players across the career:

  • Stanley Jiaye Song (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in BD (10 events, best: #2, spanning 4 seasons)
  • Marzuq Mathani (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events)
  • Leheng Xu (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, across 2 seasons)

The 2025-2026 season has been a step up, with 1 title across 6 tournaments — a clear sign that Kingsley is still ascending.

Kingsley has been on this journey through U13, U15, U17 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

kingsley_chong.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kingsley Chong, from Washington, currently competing at the U17 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-17. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Kingsley has 67 career entries across 26 tournaments and has managed to win 2 of them — a 3% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Kingsley saga began at 2021 NW CRC with 1 entries and a best finish of 9th. The first title would not come until 2024 OBA NW OLC in BS U15, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Kingsley's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Kingsley experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Kingsley has reached 3 championship matches across this career. That is 3 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 3 times walking away empty-handed. Now yes, Kingsley does own 2 actual titles, so it is not like Kingsley has never won anything. But 3 finals losses versus 2 wins means Kingsley is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kingsley plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. All 2 titles came in singles — the discipline where nobody else can mess things up. In doubles? Zero titles. In mixed? Also zero. Either Kingsley is a lone wolf who thrives in isolation, or — and this is our working theory — nobody wants to be the partner who has to explain the loss at dinner. And then there are the partners — 14 of them, to be precise. Kingsley has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Kingsley's texts. The longest-running partnership is with Stanley Jiaye Song: 10 events together, zero titles. We hope Stanley Jiaye Song has a good therapist. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals in BD U15 — we hope Yunshang Chen has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Kingsley's 2 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Kingsley has shown up 5 times with a best of top-17. Nobody is making a documentary about this. Tournament committees have handed Kingsley a #1 or #2 seed 3 times, and Kingsley has failed to live up to it in 1 of them — including at 2024 SBC NW CRC in BS U17, where Kingsley was seeded #1 and finished 4th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Kingsley is not just losing locally — Kingsley is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 3% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chong family travels cross-country so Kingsley can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kingsley has 32 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kingsley showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U13, which means Kingsley traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 SoCal ORC in BS U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 SoCal ORC, where Kingsley entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Kingsley paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 5 seasons deep, 2 titles to the name, 67 entries, and 65 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Kingsley looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Kingsley's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Kingsley has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Kingsley. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

kingston_chan.name: Kingston Chan
kingston_chan.summary: Kingston Chan has stepped onto the competitive stage with 1 tournament entry. Every champion's story has a beginning, and this is Kingston's.
kingston_chan.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kingston Chan, from parts unknown. A player with 1 career entry and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Kingston is right on schedule. It all started at 2026 Bintang NC Open Local Championships, and so far, the only thing Kingston has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Kingston saga began at 2026 Bintang NC Open Local Championships with 1 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Kingston is a singles purist — no doubles, no mixed, just one person on each side of the net and nowhere to hide. Either nobody wants to be Kingston's partner (and honestly, looking at these numbers, who could blame them), or Kingston is the kind of competitor who refuses to share credit. Admirable? Maybe. Lonely? Definitely.

Kingston is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Kingston wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Kingston, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

kirsten_young.name: Kirsten Young
kirsten_young.summary:
Out of Illinois, Kirsten Young is writing the opening pages of a badminton story, currently competing at the U15 level, with 5 entries across 2 tournaments. The willingness to compete is where every great player starts.

A true triple-threat, Kirsten competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles. Competing across all disciplines builds a well-rounded game that pure specialists can't replicate.
kirsten_young.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kirsten Young, from Illinois, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 5 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Kirsten is right on schedule. It all started at 2024 Midwest CRC, and so far, the only thing Kirsten has collected is experience — which is what people say when they have nothing else to show for it.

The Kirsten saga began at 2024 Midwest CRC with 3 entries and a best of 5th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kirsten plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 5 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kirsten has 3 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kirsten showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GS U15, which means Kirsten traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in GD U15. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC, where Kirsten entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Kirsten paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Kirsten is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Kirsten wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Kirsten, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

kiyansh_chaudhary.summary:
Out of Texas, Kiyansh Chaudhary is a consistent semifinalist, currently competing at the U15 level, reaching the final four 3 times. That kind of reliability at the top of the draw is the signature of a player who will soon be contending for titles.

Kiyansh is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting a singles best of #3, a doubles best of #4, a mixed doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (2 entries).

Kiyansh has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

With tournaments spanning Midwest, NE, South, Kiyansh isn't afraid to compete outside the home region — and that exposure to different playing styles only sharpens the game.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Kiyansh brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
kiyansh_chaudhary.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kiyansh Chaudhary, from Texas, currently competing at the U15 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-33. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Kiyansh has 22 career entries across 9 tournaments and has managed to win 0 of them — a 0% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Kiyansh saga began at 2022 South Frisco ORC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kiyansh plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 22 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 11 of them, to be precise. Kiyansh has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Kiyansh's texts. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U15 — we hope Naisha Madkaiker has emotionally recovered.

At Junior Nationals, Kiyansh has shown up 2 times with a best of top-33. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

Kiyansh has competed across 3 different regions, which means the losing is not confined to one part of the country — it is a nationally distributed phenomenon.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kiyansh has 15 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kiyansh showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 97th-place finish at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U15, which means Kiyansh traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2023 South ORC in BS U13. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Kiyansh entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 97th. That is an average placement of 65th, which is a polite way of saying Kiyansh paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Kiyansh keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Kiyansh has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Kiyansh, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Kiyansh. We will be here, taking notes.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (2 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries).

With 10 different doubles partners over the years, Kody adapts to anyone:

Kody has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Kody has competed across 5 different regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Kody brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
kody_seow.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kody Seow, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-17. Top-17 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 28 career entries in 11 tournaments, Kody has 0 titles — a 0% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 27 entries where Kody drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 28 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Kody saga began at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals with 3 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Kody plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 28 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. And then there are the partners — 10 of them, to be precise. Kody has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Kody's texts. The go-to partner is Annabelle Zhou (3 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BD U17 — we hope Michael Jiang has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Kody entered 2 events with results ranging from top-17 to a humbling round-of-33. The Selection committee looked at Kody's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Kody and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Kody has shown up 6 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Kody is not just losing locally — Kody is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Seow family travels cross-country so Kody can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2022 NorCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kody has 19 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kody showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U17, which means Kody traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in BS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC, where Kody entered 2 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 49th, which is a polite way of saying Kody paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Season after season, Kody keeps coming back — packing the bag, paying the fees, driving to gyms in cities Kody has never been to, and doing it all over again. Some people call that grit. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Kody, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Kody. We will be here, taking notes.

Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kohta Ito, from Oregon, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-13. Not terrible! Also not a championship. We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Kohta saga began at 2022 NW Oregon OLC with 1 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 4 seasons later.

At Junior Nationals, Kohta has shown up 4 times with a best of top-13. Nobody is making a documentary about this.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Kohta is not just losing locally — Kohta is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Ito family travels cross-country so Kohta can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 NW ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kohta has 12 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kohta showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 NW ORC in BS U17, which means Kohta traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals in BS U19. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Kohta entered 2 events with results ranging from 25th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 29th, which is a polite way of saying Kohta paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So here we are: 4 seasons, 15 entries, and not a single title to show for any of it. Most people would have taken a long, hard look in the mirror by now. Most people would have considered tennis, or chess, or literally any hobby with a higher return on investment than junior badminton. But not Kohta. Kohta just fills out another entry form, packs the racket bag, and drives to the next tournament with the unshakable optimism of someone who has never read their own statistics. The definition of insanity is doing the same thing over and over and expecting different results — and Kohta has turned that into a multi-year lifestyle. But you know what? The sport needs people like Kohta. Every bracket needs a first-round opponent. Every tournament needs entry fees. And every champion needs someone to beat on the way to the title. So from the bottom of our hearts, Kohta: thank you for your service. We mean that. Mostly.

koyu_tara_chou.name: Koyu(Tara) Chou
koyu_tara_chou.summary:
Hailing from California, Koyu(Tara) Chou stands as an ORC champion, capturing an impressive 5 ORC titles across 4 regional championships. ORCs are the highest level of regular-season competition, drawing the strongest fields in each region. Winning at this level repeatedly marks Koyu(Tara) as one of the premier players on the circuit.

Koyu(Tara) is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 2 singles titles, 2 doubles titles, 2 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: 5 ORC titles.

Koyu(Tara) has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 9 different players across the career:
  • Daniel Zhou (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events, best: #2)
  • Chloe Ho (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in GD

The 2022-2023 season has been outstanding, with 6 podium finishes across 3 tournaments. Koyu(Tara) is playing some of the best badminton of the career right now.

Koyu(Tara) has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Koyu(Tara) has been seeded #1 or #2 in 6 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Now in the 2nd season of competition, Koyu(Tara)'s journey is gathering momentum with every tournament. The future is bright.
koyu_tara_chou.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Koyu(Tara) Chou, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. An Open Regional Championship titlist with 5 ORC titles, which sounds very official — and it is, ORCs are the real deal. But let's zoom out: that's 5 wins out of 17 ORC entries, and an overall career of 6-for-19 (32%). Regional royalty, national afterthought. A big deal on the local circuit, a footnote everywhere else. Let's dig in.

The Koyu(Tara) saga began at 2021 NorCal CRC, where Koyu(Tara) burst onto the scene with a title in XD U19 — a debut that promised great things. And to be fair, Koyu(Tara) has delivered on some of those promises: 6 total titles is nothing to sneeze at. But the gap between what Koyu(Tara) promised and what Koyu(Tara) has delivered is wide enough to park a minivan in. The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 5 titles in 11 entries — a stretch where Koyu(Tara) actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. This season? 1 win in 8 entries. The trajectory is... let's call it 'humbling.'

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Koyu(Tara) experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Koyu(Tara) has reached 6 championship matches across this career. That is 6 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 6 times walking away empty-handed. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (4 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Koyu(Tara) does own 6 actual titles, so it is not like Koyu(Tara) has never won anything. But 6 finals losses versus 6 wins means Koyu(Tara) is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Koyu(Tara) plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 2 doubles, 2 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Koyu(Tara) can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Koyu(Tara) has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Koyu(Tara)'s texts. The go-to partner is Andrew S Wang (3 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-17 exit at 2023 SoCal ORC in GD U19 — we hope Wenlu She has emotionally recovered.

Tournament committees have handed Koyu(Tara) a #1 or #2 seed 6 times, and Koyu(Tara) has failed to live up to it in 2 of them — including at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC in GS U19, where Koyu(Tara) was seeded #2 and finished 5th. Being the top seed and still losing is like being the teacher's pet and still failing the exam.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Koyu(Tara) is not just losing locally — Koyu(Tara) is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 32% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Chou family travels cross-country so Koyu(Tara) can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-17 finish at 2023 SoCal ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Koyu(Tara) has 2 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Koyu(Tara) showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2023 SoCal ORC in GS U19, which means Koyu(Tara) traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-17 at 2023 SoCal ORC in GD U19. A pattern, not a blip.

So where does this leave us? 2 seasons deep, 6 titles to the name, 19 entries, and 13 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the ORC victories, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Koyu(Tara) looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Koyu(Tara)'s side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Koyu(Tara) has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Koyu(Tara). The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

Kriday competes in both singles and doubles, developing the kind of well-rounded game that pays dividends as competition intensifies at higher levels.

Kriday has competed at both U13 and U15, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

kriday_ashok.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kriday Ashok, from parts unknown, currently competing at the U15 level. A player with 5 career entries and zero wins. The journey of a thousand losses begins with a single entry form, and Kriday is right on schedule. The Kriday saga began at 2022 Synergy NorCal ORC with 2 entries and a best of 17th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 3 seasons later.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Kriday has 5 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Kriday showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. We will be here, taking notes.

krish_ashok.name: Krish Ashok
krish_ashok.summary:
Hailing from California, Krish Ashok has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U19 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Krish's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Krish has entered higher age groups at 1 tournament, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

A true triple-threat, Krish competes across singles, doubles, and mixed doubles — boasting 2 singles titles, 1 mixed doubles title. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: U.S. Selection Event participant (3 entries) • Junior Nationals competitor (8 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

Krish has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 16 different players across the career:
  • Daby Huang (current primary partner) — 4 events together this season alone with 1 title, building serious momentum (5 total events in XD, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Abhi Mandanapu (current partner) — currently teaming up in BD (2 events, best: #6, across 2 seasons)

Having moved up to U19, Krish is navigating tougher competition at the higher age group. The adjustment is natural — and with the pedigree Krish brings, a return to the top is a matter of when, not if.

A veteran of the junior circuit, Krish has competed across U15, U17 and U19, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Krish has competed across 6 different regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 4 seasons of competitive experience and 3 titles, Krish is a battle-hardened competitor whose journey is a testament to dedication, talent, and love for the game.
krish_ashok.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Krish Ashok, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. This is a player who has competed at the U.S. Selection Event — the single most prestigious tournament in American junior badminton, the one where you play for the right to represent your country — and finished top-9. Top-9 at Selection. So close to the ultimate prize, and yet so far. Across 41 career entries in 16 tournaments, Krish has 3 titles — a 7% conversion rate. That means for every win, there are about 12 entries where Krish drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 41 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

The Krish saga began at 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals with 2 entries and a best finish of 33rd. The first title would not come until 2025 OBA NW OLC in BS U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Krish's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2024-2025 season — 3 titles in 18 entries — a stretch where Krish actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Krish is 0-for-7. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Krish plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 2 singles, 0 doubles, 1 mixed title. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Krish can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 16 of them, to be precise. Krish has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Krish's texts. The go-to partner is Daby Huang (5 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-65 exit at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17 — we hope Sriya Sriganesh has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Krish's 3 titles came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Krish entered 3 events with results ranging from top-9 to a humbling round-of-17. The Selection committee looked at Krish's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Krish and said 'nah.' At Junior Nationals, Krish has shown up 8 times with a best of top-6. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-6.

Krish has also played up into older age groups at 1 tournament, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Krish is not just losing locally — Krish is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 6 regions (Midwest, NE, NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 7% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Ashok family travels cross-country so Krish can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Krish has 23 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Krish showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2024 Frisco South ORC in BS U17, which means Krish traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-65 at 2024 Frisco South ORC in XD U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Krish entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 54th, which is a polite way of saying Krish paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 3 titles to the name, 41 entries, and 38 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Selection Event appearance, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Krish looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Krish's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Krish has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Krish. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

krish_tennety.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Krish Tennety, from California, currently competing at the U13 level. A player who has entered 10 events across 4 tournaments and has won exactly zero of them. Not one. We checked twice. This is not a typo, and it is not bad luck — it is a 10-event streak that statisticians would describe as 'impressively consistent at not winning.' Krish's trophy case is so empty it echoes. And yet, Krish keeps coming back, which is either the most inspiring thing we have ever seen or a cry for help. We genuinely cannot tell.

The Krish saga began at 2022 NW ORC with 2 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Krish plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 10 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero. On the doubles front, Krish has cycled through 5 different partners, which is either a sign of someone constantly searching for the perfect fit or — more likely — a sign that the partners keep finding excuses to be 'unavailable.' The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2023 South ORC in BD U13 — we hope Daniel Yan has emotionally recovered.

And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Krish is not just losing locally — Krish is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (NW, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 0% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Tennety family travels cross-country so Krish can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-65 finish at 2023 South ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Krish has 9 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Krish showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 65th-place finish at 2023 South ORC in BS U13, which means Krish traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 NorCal ORC in BS U11. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2023 South ORC, where Krish entered 3 events with results ranging from 33rd to 65th. That is an average placement of 43rd, which is a polite way of saying Krish paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

Krish is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Krish wants to be. Or — and this is statistically more likely — time to accumulate a much longer and more detailed record of losing, which will make for an even better roast in two years. Either way, we are invested now. Welcome to junior badminton, Krish, where the shuttles are fast, the parents are intense, and the entry fees never stop. We will be watching.

krisha_tyagi.name: Krisha Tyagi
krisha_tyagi.summary:
Hailing from Georgia, Krisha Tyagi has reached the podium at the ORC level (top-4), currently competing at the U19 level, competing against the best players in the region. Open Regional Championships are a significant step up from local events, and Krisha's results show the readiness for top-tier competition.

Notably, Krisha has entered higher age groups at 2 tournaments, voluntarily taking on older competition — a bold move that reveals both confidence and a hunger for growth.

Krisha is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 1 singles title, a doubles best of #4. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Junior Nationals competitor (6 entries) • ORC top-4 finisher.

Krisha has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 9 different players across the career:
  • Louis N Thai (current primary partner) — currently teaming up in XD (4 events, best: #5, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Arya Prakash Mirani (current partner) — currently teaming up in XD (2 events)

Krisha has competed at both U17 and U19, showing the adaptability needed to thrive as competition grows fiercer with each age group.

Krisha has competed across 4 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

With 3 seasons of competitive experience, Krisha brings tournament-tested composure that only comes from years of stepping onto the court against the best.
krisha_tyagi.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Krisha Tyagi, from Georgia, currently competing at the U19 level. This is someone who has been to Junior Nationals — the biggest stage in American junior badminton — and came home with a best finish of top-9. Not terrible! Also not a championship. Krisha has 27 career entries across 11 tournaments and has managed to win 1 of them — a 4% rate that screams 'perennial contender who cannot close.' We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Krisha saga began at 2021 NE ORC (U15-U19) with 3 entries and a best finish of 4th. The first title would not come until 2022 South Houston CRC in GS U17, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Krisha's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' The peak came during the 2021-2022 season — 1 title in 13 entries — a stretch where Krisha actually looked like the player everyone hoped for. Fast forward to this season and Krisha is 0-for-9. The glory days are not just over — they are actively running in the other direction.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Krisha plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. All 1 title came in singles — the discipline where nobody else can mess things up. In doubles? Zero titles. In mixed? Also zero. Either Krisha is a lone wolf who thrives in isolation, or — and this is our working theory — nobody wants to be the partner who has to explain the loss at dinner. And then there are the partners — 9 of them, to be precise. Krisha has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Krisha's texts. The go-to partner is Rishita Tubati (4 events, 0 wins), which is the definition of diminishing returns. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U17 — we hope David Purnomo has emotionally recovered.

Now, about the quality of those wins. Every single one of Krisha's 1 title came at local events — OLCs and CRCs — where the field is small, the competition is friendly, and the biggest obstacle is finding parking. At the ORC level and above, where the elite show up and the brackets get serious? Zero titles. It is the competitive equivalent of being the tallest kid in kindergarten and thinking you are ready for the NBA. At Junior Nationals, Krisha has shown up 6 times with a best of top-9. Respectable, but nobody remembers who finished top-9.

Krisha has also played up into older age groups at 2 tournaments, which is the badminton equivalent of a sophomore crashing the senior prom — bold, admirable, and almost always ending in tears. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Krisha is not just losing locally — Krisha is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 4 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 4% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Tyagi family travels cross-country so Krisha can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Krisha has 13 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Krisha showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2022 SPBA Midwest ORC in XD U17, which means Krisha traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. And it was not a one-time thing — there was also a round-of-33 at 2022 South Frisco ORC in GS U17. A pattern, not a blip. The single worst tournament experience was 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Krisha entered 2 events with results ranging from 17th to 33rd. That is an average placement of 25th, which is a polite way of saying Krisha paid full price to get absolutely demolished.

So where does this leave us? 3 seasons deep, 1 title to the name, 27 entries, and 26 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the local titles, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Krisha looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who has a complicated relationship with winning, keeps searching for the right doubles partner like a bad dating app, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Krisha's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Krisha has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Krisha. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

We are sure the 'I almost had it at Nationals' story absolutely kills at family dinners, right between the appetizer and that look of quiet disappointment from the parent who drove nine hours to watch two matches.

The Krishna saga began at 2023 NW OLC with 1 entries and a best of 9th, and somehow the winning has not started yet — 1 seasons later.

The single worst tournament experience was 2023 U.S. Junior Nationals, where Krishna entered 2 events with results ranging from 25th to 65th. Krishna is just getting started on the circuit, which means there is still time. Time to turn this around, time to find a groove, time to become the player the entry form says Krishna wants to be. krishnavi_kasu.name: Krishnavi Kasu
krishnavi_kasu.summary:
Hailing from Georgia, Krishnavi Kasu is a Pan American Junior Championships bronze medalist, earning a podium finish representing the USA against the best young players in the Western Hemisphere (GS U13). A top-3 finish at the Pan Am Juniors puts Krishnavi in rarified international company.

Krishnavi is known for playing up, regularly entering higher age groups and testing against older, more experienced opponents (4 tournaments with multi-age-group entries). That willingness to seek out tougher competition accelerates development in ways that playing it safe never can.

Krishnavi is that rare three-discipline competitor — singles, doubles, and mixed — boasting 10 singles titles, 7 doubles titles, 3 mixed doubles titles. The ability to excel in all three formats reveals both individual brilliance and the court sense to thrive alongside a partner.

Career highlights: Pan Am Junior bronze medalist (GS U13, 2025) • U.S. Selection Event top-3 (U13 GS) • Junior Nationals champion (XD U11, GD U11) • 10 ORC titles.

The statistics paint a vivid picture — Krishnavi boasts a career +1195 point differential, 66 straight-games victories, showing the ability to close out matches decisively, a clutch 73% win rate in three-game matches (22/30).

Krishnavi has shown remarkable doubles adaptability, partnering with 12 different players across the career:
  • Claire QiuChen Li (current primary partner) — 11 events together this season alone with 4 titles, building serious momentum (13 total events in GD/U13, spanning 3 seasons)
  • Anirudh Rudharraju (current primary partner) — 13 events, 1 title together in XD, spanning 3 seasons
  • Ethan Yin (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in XD
  • Medha Sivani Gudavalli (current partner) — 1 events, 1 title together in GD

The 2025-2026 season has been a step up, with 9 titles across 6 tournaments — a clear sign that Krishnavi is still ascending.

Krishnavi has been on this journey through U11, U13, U15 — a multi-age-group career that few can match, accumulating invaluable experience at every level. That depth of competitive history is an asset younger opponents simply cannot replicate.

Krishnavi has competed across 5 different regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South), demonstrating a willingness to travel the country and test the game against diverse competition from coast to coast.

Krishnavi has been seeded #1 or #2 in 16 events — recognition by tournament committees that this player consistently ranks among the very best in the draw.

Over 4 seasons and 20 titles, Krishnavi has built a legacy that speaks volumes about what sustained effort and competitive fire can achieve.
krishnavi_kasu.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Krishnavi Kasu, from Georgia, currently competing at the U13 level. Now, we need to address the elephant in the room: Krishnavi is a Pan American Junior Championships bronze medalist (GS U13), which means Krishnavi has literally represented the United States of America on the international stage and come home with hardware. That is genuinely impressive, and Krishnavi's parents have probably mentioned it to every relative, neighbor, and grocery store cashier within a 50-mile radius. But here is the thing about a bronze medal: it means Krishnavi went all the way to an international championship and STILL did not win. Krishnavi represented an entire country and came home with the consolation prize. Across 71 career entries and a 28% win rate, the Pan Am bronze is the crown jewel of a résumé that is otherwise... well, let's just say we have 71 entries worth of material.

The Krishnavi saga began at 2023 South ORC with 2 entries and a best finish of 5th. The first title would not come until 2023 South CRC in GS U11, and you could almost hear the collective sigh of relief from Krishnavi's family, who had been patiently attending tournaments and pretending it was 'all about the experience.' This 2025-2026 season has actually been the best yet — 9 titles in 17 entries — which really tells you everything about how low the bar was before.

But nothing — absolutely nothing — defines the Krishnavi experience quite like the finals record. Let us walk you through this, because the numbers alone do not do it justice. Krishnavi has reached 9 championship matches across this career. That is 9 times standing one single match away from a title — the bracket won, the semifinal opponent dispatched, the crowd watching, the trophy right there — and 9 times walking away empty-handed. Take 2024 Frisco South ORC, where Krishnavi entered 3 events and came away with 2 runner-up finishes (XD U11, GD U11). 2 chances at gold, 2 silver medals. That is not bad luck — that is an IDENTITY. It happens in singles (2 finals losses), and it happens in doubles and mixed (7 more). This is not a discipline-specific problem — this is a spiritual condition. Now yes, Krishnavi does own 20 actual titles, so it is not like Krishnavi has never won anything. But 9 finals losses versus 20 wins means Krishnavi is literally more likely to choke in a championship match than to win one. The clutch gene is not missing — it's just on an extremely unreliable part-time schedule, like a substitute teacher who shows up whenever they feel like it.

Let us talk about the discipline split, because this is where the story gets interesting. Krishnavi plays singles, doubles, AND mixed doubles, which on paper looks like the résumé of a well-rounded, versatile athlete. On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. The breakdown: 10 singles, 7 doubles, 3 mixed titles. Jack of all trades and a master of — well, let us be generous and leave that sentence unfinished. The point is, Krishnavi can technically win in any format, just not often enough for anyone to be truly impressed. And then there are the partners — 12 of them, to be precise. Krishnavi has burned through doubles partners the way some people burn through Netflix shows: binge, lose interest, move on. The revolving door spins so fast it is a miracle anyone still answers Krishnavi's texts. The go-to partner is Anirudh Rudharraju (13 events, 1 win), which is not exactly setting the world on fire. The doubles lowlight? A round-of-33 exit at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U13 — we hope Taneesh Musunuru has emotionally recovered.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Krishnavi entered 3 events with results ranging from top-3 to a humbling round-of-5. The Selection committee looked at Krishnavi's record and saw enough talent to deserve an invitation. The bracket looked at Krishnavi and said 'nah.' Then there is the seeding situation. Krishnavi has been seeded in 21 events and underperformed the seeding 6 times. A seed is supposed to be a badge of honor — tournament committees studying the field, running the numbers, and saying 'this player belongs at the top of the bracket.' Krishnavi takes that badge and promptly sets it on fire. The worst offense: seeded #1 at 2024 South OLC (Nets & Turf) in GS U13, and finishing 9th. That is a seeded player losing to multiple unseeded opponents. The committee believed in Krishnavi. The evidence suggests they should not have.

One thing nobody can accuse Krishnavi of is playing it safe. Krishnavi has entered older age groups at 4 different tournaments, spanning U11, U13, U15 — which means Krishnavi has voluntarily walked into brackets full of bigger, older, more experienced players and said 'yes, I would like to compete against these people who have been alive longer than me.' And incredibly, 12 of those playing-up attempts ended in a title, which takes the insanity from pure to impressive. There is a fine line between 'fearless competitor who seeks out the toughest challenges' and 'person who genuinely enjoys getting bodied by bigger kids,' and Krishnavi has built a permanent residence on that line. And let us talk about the travel schedule, because Krishnavi is not just losing locally — Krishnavi is losing NATIONALLY. Tournaments across 5 regions (Midwest, NE, NorCal, SoCal, South) means airplane tickets, hotel rooms, rental cars, tournament entry fees, and the kind of family logistics that require a spreadsheet and a prayer. All in pursuit of a 28% win rate. Most families travel cross-country to visit the Grand Canyon or Disney World. The Kasu family travels cross-country so Krishnavi can lose at badminton in exciting new zip codes. The crown jewel of the travel schedule? A round-of-33 finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai. Hope the hotel at least had a pool.

We would be doing a disservice to the art of the roast if we did not address the lowlights. Krishnavi has 1 career results of round-of-17 or worse — tournaments where Krishnavi showed up, played maybe one match, lost, and went home before the gym even got properly warm. The absolute rock bottom was a 33rd-place finish at 2025 DFW South ORC / Shonai in XD U13, which means Krishnavi traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet.

So where does this leave us? 4 seasons deep, 20 titles to the name, 71 entries, and 51 losses. If you squint at the highlights — the Pan Am medal, the big wins, the moments of genuine brilliance — Krishnavi looks like a player on the rise. But we did not squint. We pulled up every single entry, every draw, every result, and what we found is a player who chokes in finals like it is a hobby, treats seeds like suggestions, and travels the country paying entry fees with the enthusiasm of someone who truly believes the next tournament will be different. And maybe it will be. But probably not. The data is not on Krishnavi's side, but the human spirit is a powerful thing, and Krishnavi has enough of it to power a small city. So keep swinging, Krishnavi. The shuttle tube industry thanks you. The tournament organizers thank you. The opponents who padded their records against you thank you most of all. We will be watching.

kristine_nicole_ngo.roast:
Ladies and gentlemen, put your hands together for Kristine Nicole Ngo, from California, currently competing at the U19 level. That means for every win, there are about 9 entries where Kristine drove to the gym, paid the fee, warmed up, competed, lost, and drove home. So buckle in, because we have 10 entries worth of material, and we intend to use every last one of them.

On closer inspection, it looks more like the résumé of someone who keeps trying different formats hoping one of them will work. Three disciplines, 10 entries, zero titles in any format. That is not versatility — that is just expanding the portfolio of failure across multiple asset classes. Diversification does not help when every investment returns zero.

At the Selection Event — where the stakes are as high as they get in American junior badminton — Kristine entered 2 events with results ranging from top-4 to a humbling round-of-17. The absolute rock bottom was a 17th-place finish at 2021 U.S. Junior Nationals in GS U19, which means Kristine traveled to a tournament, registered, warmed up, stepped on court, and was eliminated so quickly that the ink on the draw sheet was still wet. Some people call that stubbornness. Some people call that a refusal to read a spreadsheet. Whatever it is, the junior circuit would genuinely not be the same without Kristine, and we mean that in every possible interpretation of the sentence. Keep swinging, Kristine. We will be here, taking notes.
